items.rationale: His core thesis: decentralized systems outperform centralized ones, eliminate information asymmetries, and form the settlement layer of the next internet — builders should design for it deliberately.
items.age_note: Stated repeatedly 2021–2022; most recent restatement Nov 2022, so fully current.

items.rationale: Believes DAOs are fundamental to web3 and a groundbreaking coordination tool, but need domestic US legal wrappers (UNAs) to solve liability, tax, and contracting problems.
items.age_note: Excitement tempered by mid-2022 — he urged expectations be 'adjusted downwards,' viewing most DAOs as city governments not startups.

items.rationale: Sees DEXs as critical web3 infrastructure enabling interoperability across the whole ecosystem, and judges most decentralized DeFi primitives to have performed well through volatility.
Excited about NFTs broadly and led the Can't Be Evil license suite to reduce legal ambiguity, protect creator IP, and unlock the economic potential of NFT communities.
Believes decentralizing client/app layers above the blockchain turns platforms into public-utility-like infrastructure and will retrospectively be obvious; backed Farcaster.
Personal passion (gaming background); sees tokenization of games as a new norm and an expanding design space, working closely with the a16z games team.
Argues collateral quality (exogenous over endogenous) — not algorithms — is the key risk lever, and well-collateralized stablecoins can power global commerce and dollar dominance in web3.
Views privacy-preserving tech as one of the most important and complex regulatory/legal frontiers in web3; personally excited about what ZK proofs make possible.
Sees tokens as enabling an economic paradigm impossible with shares, and considers evolving tokenomics one of the most interesting active subjects in web3.
His single loudest concern: opaque enforcement without guidance is slow, unfair to good actors, drives builders offshore, and has failed to prevent fraud.
items.age_note: Consistent theme since 2021; sharpened post-FTX into a flat 'it has failed' verdict by Nov–Dec 2022.

items.rationale: Fears regulators applying compliance burdens to autonomous protocols/DAOs (CFTC v. Ooki, DCCPA) would make decentralization impossible and effectively end web3; insists regulation belongs at the app/business layer.
Views centralized intermediaries (FTX, Celsius, 3AC) and fraud as the true source of recent collapses and the biggest ongoing risk to web3 users — distinct from trustless DeFi.
items.age_note: Crystallized after FTX in Nov 2022; fraud-as-top-risk view persisted from early 2022.

items.rationale: Considers undercollateralized stablecoins relying on endogenous collateral the riskiest segment and root cause of collapses like Terra; dismisses them as valueless.
Worries the hostile US environment pushes founders abroad, reducing US oversight and increasing risk; wants the US to attract rather than repel builders.
Repeatedly warns that selling tokens to US persons for fundraising is the clearest securities-law violation, and airdrops are far safer; wary of recurring 'non-dilutive financing' pitches.
items.age_note: Persistent stance from Dec 2021 through Jul 2022; never reversed.

items.rationale: Concerned that censoring protocols sets a precedent for other governments to do the same, producing a geopolitically encumbered rather than open internet.
Warns that unwrapped DAOs expose members/multisig holders to unlimited liability and litigation (e.g., Merit Circle tortious interference), and expects the problem to worsen before it improves.
Argues ETH is sufficiently decentralized that no managerial efforts are relied upon, so it should not be a security — including post-merge under proof-of-stake.
Holds up Bitcoin as the clear example of a trustless, decentralized, non-security asset with minimal information asymmetry.
Defends Maker/DAI's exogenous collateral model as fundamentally safer than Terra; cites USDC backing as stabilizing rather than weakening it.
Groups Frax with DAI as legitimate decentralized/algorithmic stablecoins that proposed legislation does not (and should not) outlaw.
Holds up the ENS launch as the model for a great, non-underwriting token launch where the DAO (not the dev corp) handles market activities.
items.age_note: Stated Dec 2021 and not restated since; cited as a persisted, older endorsement.

items.rationale: His canonical example of failure: undercollateralized, reliant on endogenous collateral, not decentralized — the root cause being bad collateral, not just the algorithm.
Cites FTT as widely viewed with skepticism in the legal community and as a non-decentralized token tied to a centralized fraud (FTX).
Believes XRP lacks a credible decentralization claim for the period Ripple sold it to US retail, making it vulnerable on the securities question (though outcome won't generalize to today's issuers).

risk_regime.rationale: Jennings is a legal/policy lead, not a markets allocator, so he expresses conviction in decentralized web3's structural future rather than near-term price direction. He is structurally optimistic — telling founders to keep building and framing post-FTX 2022 as an opportunity for the US to lead — but tactically cautious: he expects DAO/legal problems and the market to get 'worse before they get better,' and sees regulatory uncertainty plus CeFi/fraud blowups as acute near-term risks. Net posture is balanced: long-term constructive, short-term guarded.

notes: Jennings' corpus is overwhelmingly legal/regulatory rather than investment-thesis driven, so 'excitement' and 'concern' are inferred from his policy advocacy and his framing of which structures/assets are durable. His unifying lens is decentralization and information-asymmetry: assets/sectors that are trustless and decentralized are framed positively (defensible, non-security, durable), while centralized intermediaries, uncollateralized stablecoins, and fundraising token sales are framed as risky. Recency weighting: post-FTX (Nov–Dec 2022) statements harden his anti-regulation-by-enforcement and pro-decentralization stances and sharpen the CeFi-vs-DeFi distinction; no major reversals were found — his views are remarkably consistent across the 18-month span, mostly intensifying rather than changing. The ENS endorsement is the main older (2021) view not restated. Token-level views are expressed through a securities-law lens, not a price/return lens.
